one_line_takeaway: SHADOWMERGE shows that a single ordinary user message, engineered via the Anchor-Inscribe-Render pipeline to create a channel-aligned conflicting relation, can reliably poison shared Mem0/Graphiti graph-based agent memory (93.8% average ASR with negligible utility loss), and that surface-level write-time rephrasing defenses fail to stop it because the attack operates at the relation-channel level, not the text level.
key_finding: SHADOWMERGE achieves 0.938 average (macro) ASR across the three datasets while preserving 0.973 average benign-task utility, versus a clean/no-attack utility baseline of 0.984. This is a 50.3-percentage-point absolute macro-ASR gain over the strongest adapted baseline (GRAGPoison-adapt at 0.435 macro ASR; Naive Text Poisoning 0.289; MINJA-adapt 0.293). Per-dataset ASR: PubMedQA 0.945, WebShop 0.900, ToolEmu 0.970 (gains of 59.5, 17.5, and 42.0 points respectively over the best baseline on each dataset). Mechanism study: SHADOWMERGE keeps materialization/merge/retrieval/ASR at 0.958/0.958/0.948/0.938, versus GRAGPoison-adapt's 0.908/0.908/0.579/0.435 -- the largest gap is at retrieval, i.e. baselines can write poisoned content but fail to get it returned as graph evidence for the target query. Rank quality: across 534 ranked poisoned retrievals, mean rank 1.169 (median 1.0), rank 1 in 91.6% of cases and top-3 in 98.1%, versus GRAGPoison-adapt's mean rank 2.431 and only 26.2% rank-1 hits. AIR ablation on the 10-anchor subsample: removing Anchor, Inscribe, or Render drops macro ASR from 0.913 (full) to 0.567, 0.540, and 0.553 respectively -- each stage is independently necessary. Model-role sensitivity: swapping the target-reasoning model to DeepSeek-V4 Pro or Gemini-3.1 Pro keeps ASR high (0.967, 0.953 vs. 0.913 reference); swapping the graph-extraction model moves ASR more (0.860 for DeepSeek-V4 Pro, 0.960 for Gemini-3.1 Pro), and three different judge models (Claude Sonnet 4.6, DeepSeek-V4 Pro, Gemini-3.1 Pro) agree on 0.900 aggregate ASR. Background-write sensitivity: ASR stays in 0.900-0.973 as background writes increase from 20 to 40. Backend transfer: on Graphiti (instead of Mem0) SHADOWMERGE still achieves 0.800 ASR with unchanged benign utility, though merge/retrieval rates drop from 1.000 to 0.800. Defense test: a representative write-time semantic-preserving rephrasing defense leaves macro ASR unchanged at 0.913 (per-dataset deltas of +0.020 PubMedQA, -0.100 WebShop, +0.080 ToolEmu), because it preserves the anchor, relation channel, and conflicting value that the graph pipeline extracts even after surface rewording.
core_contribution: The paper presents SHADOWMERGE, a black-box, query-only poisoning attack against shared graph-based agent memory (evaluated on Mem0 and, for a backend check, Graphiti). The key idea, formalized as Channel-Aligned Relational Competition (CARC), is that a poisoned relation can be crafted to share the same query-activated anchor entity and the same canonicalized 'relation channel' as an existing benign relation, while carrying a conflicting value, so that the graph-memory pipeline extracts, merges, and later retrieves it alongside (and competing with) the benign evidence. The attack is realized via a three-stage attacker-side pipeline called AIR: Anchor (selects a query-reachable entity likely to be merged/retrieved, using an attacker-built 'shadow co-activation graph' and an Anchor Influence Maximization scoring function), Inscribe (Schema-Aligned Counterfactual Inscription: builds a channel-aligned conflicting relation using a shadow schema dictionary and a trained KGE model to pick a sibling relation type and a conflicting value), and Render (Retrieval-Margin Camouflage: a genetic/local-search procedure over payload phrasings guided by public retrieval, perplexity, and anomaly proxies to keep the natural-language payload extractable, retrievable, and plausible). The entire attack requires only submitting one ordinary user message through the standard agent interface; the attacker never inspects or edits the memory graph, retriever, or extraction logs directly.
threat_model: Ordinary-interaction, query-only black-box access: the attacker has only a regular user account on a platform with shared graph-based agent memory, and submits normal user messages through the same interface, safety checks, and memory-update path as benign users. The attacker knows the platform uses shared graph-based memory, can identify a target query and infer likely anchors/benign reference outputs from public knowledge or task templates, but does not know the current graph state, the deployed extraction model, entity-resolution thresholds, relation-canonicalization rules, retriever parameters, or other users' private interactions. Explicitly out of scope: training-time poisoning, direct graph-write attacks, memory-backend compromise, authentication bypass, cross-user data exfiltration, or attacks requiring modification of the victim's own query.
limitations: Stated by the authors: SHADOWMERGE assumes the attacker can formulate a target query and infer a plausible target anchor from public knowledge, observable task patterns, or application semantics -- it does not address settings where anchors/tasks are entirely unavailable to the attacker. The work focuses on a single poisoned relation around one target anchor; multi-anchor payloads, chained-relation poisoning, and adaptive attacks against graph-aware defenses are left as future work. The proposed graph-aware defense directions (edge-level provenance, writer-authority checks, relation-level conflict resolution, cross-user visibility policies, retrieval-time consistency checking) are explicitly framed as design directions, not evaluated defenses -- their latency, false-positive rate, and benign-utility impact are not studied. Only one input-side defense (semantic-preserving rephrasing) was actually tested; the paper does not claim all defenses fail, only that this representative surface-level one does not. Evaluation uses a single main graph-memory backend (Mem0) with only a smaller-scale, single-dataset (ToolEmu) transfer check on Graphiti. Attacker-side proxies (extraction, retrieval, KGE, perplexity, anomaly) are approximations of the deployed stack, not guarantees, which the paper itself frames as a 'proxy gap' that can lower expected success under greater proxy-deployment mismatch, though ablation and sensitivity results are only reported on a 10-anchor subsample per dataset.

datasets_used: PubMedQA (medical/clinical QA assistant, 120 sampled examples, 40 anchors)
WebShop (shopping recommendation assistant, 120 sampled examples, 40 anchors)
ToolEmu (enterprise tool-use assistant, high-risk scenarios filtered, 99 examples, 33 anchors)
quantitative_results: SHADOWMERGE macro ASR 0.938 vs. benign-utility 0.973 (clean baseline utility 0.984)
50.3 absolute percentage-point macro-ASR gain over best adapted baseline (GRAGPoison-adapt 0.435)
Mechanism study: materialized/merged/retrieved/ASR = 0.958/0.958/0.948/0.938 for SHADOWMERGE vs. 0.908/0.908/0.579/0.435 for GRAGPoison-adapt
Poisoned-evidence rank: mean 1.169, median 1.0, rank-1 in 91.6%, top-3 in 98.1% (534 ranked retrievals), vs. GRAGPoison-adapt mean rank 2.431, rank-1 in 26.2%
AIR ablation (10-anchor subsample): full reference 0.913 macro ASR; Naive Anchor 0.567 (-0.346); Template Conflict 0.540 (-0.373); Paraphrase Payload 0.553 (-0.360)

Model-role sensitivity: target-reasoning swap to DeepSeek-V4 Pro 0.967, Gemini-3.1 Pro 0.953 (vs GPT-5.5 reference 0.913); graph-extraction swap to DeepSeek-V4 Pro 0.860, Gemini-3.1 Pro 0.960; judge-model replay stable at 0.900 across Claude Sonnet 4.6, DeepSeek-V4 Pro, Gemini-3.1 Pro
Graphiti backend check (ToolEmu subsample): 0.800 ASR, unchanged benign utility, merge/retrieval rates 0.800 (vs. 1.000 on Mem0)
Write-time rephrasing defense: macro ASR unchanged at 0.913 (PubMedQA +0.020, WebShop -0.100, ToolEmu +0.080), average utility rose from 0.960 to 0.993
baselines_compared: Naive Text Poisoning (plain natural-language false claim) -- 0.289 macro ASR
MINJA-adapt (query-only memory injection, no AIR anchor/channel/rendering) -- 0.293 macro ASR
GRAGPoison-adapt (GraphRAG poisoning intent adapted to ordinary shared-memory writes) -- 0.435 macro ASR
